journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Feasibility, effectiveness and patient experience of virtual reality technology in intensive care units patients: A mixed-methods systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aSchool of Nursing, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, Hunan, 410013, China

bDepartment of Emergency Medicine, Clinical Research Center for Emergency and Critical Care in Hunan Province, Hunan Provincial Institute of Emergency Medicine, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Emergency and Critical Care Metabonomics, Hunan Provincial People's Hospital, The First Affiliated Hospital of Hunan Normal University, Changsha, Hunan, 410002, China

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Adults in critical care units endure intense anxiety, pain and functional decline; novel adjunctive therapies are urgently needed. Virtual reality has shown promise as a non-pharmacological intervention, yet its feasibility, effectiveness and patient experience require comprehensive synthesis.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To integrate quantitative and qualitative evidence on the feasibility, effectiveness and experience of virtual reality among critically ill adults.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Adhering to PRISMA guidelines, we systematically searched multiple databases from inception to March 19, 2025, for quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-methods studies on virtual reality interventions in adult intensive care unit patients. A convergent segregated approach was employed: quantitative data were narratively synthesized, qualitative data were thematically analyzed, and both sets of evidence were integrated to develop an in-depth understanding of virtual reality application.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Nineteen studies were included. Quantitative evidence indicated that virtual reality was generally safe, with no serious adverse events reported. Regarding effectiveness, virtual reality interventions effectively alleviate patient anxiety, improve sleep quality, and show positive effects in promoting early functional recovery and cognitive function. However, the effect of virtual reality on pain was heterogeneous. Qualitative data revealed high patient acceptance and satisfaction with the virtual reality experience, noting that it provided relaxation and pleasure, and enhanced a sense of autonomy and environmental control. Nevertheless, some patients reported issues such as device discomfort and the need for optimization in intervention duration and content.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Virtual reality technology serves as a useful adjunct tool for improving psychological distress and functional recovery in intensive care unit patients, with overall good safety and patient acceptance. However, its successful implementation is not merely a technical issue but a complex process requiring precise alignment with individual needs and overcoming human–device interaction barriers. Future research should focus on developing personalized virtual reality interventions and exploring optimal implementation strategies in real-world intensive care unit settings.

boxed-text.p:
What is already known about the topic?•
Virtual reality has been identified as a technology with potential benefits for both patients and healthcare professionals.•
Virtual reality shows promise as a non-pharmacological intervention for reducing anxiety and pain in critical care settings.

What this paper adds•
This review demonstrates that virtual reality effectively alleviates intensive care unit patient anxiety and improves sleep quality, while showing positive effects on functional and cognitive recovery.•
The findings reveal high patient acceptance of virtual reality in intensive care units despite device comfort issues, highlighting the need for personalized interventions and optimized implementation strategies.

notes.p: Alt-text: Unlabelled box dummy alt text

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Patients in the intensive care unit frequently face a multitude of physiological and psychological challenges, including delirium, anxiety, depression, pain, and physical dysfunction, which significantly impede their recovery and quality of life (Lewis et al., 2025). Conventional intensive care unit therapies and nursing interventions, while effective in stabilizing vital signs, often fall short in addressing the psychological and cognitive sequelae of critical illness. Furthermore, certain invasive procedures may inadvertently contribute to additional traumatic stress and iatrogenic harm (Subramaniam et al., 2024). Against this backdrop, there is a pressing need to explore innovative adjunctive therapies that can deliver integrated mind-body interventions to mitigate these multifaceted challenges.

Virtual reality technology is an advanced technology that creates a three-dimensional artificial environment, immersing users and enabling interaction with the virtual world through various sensory devices (Lyu et al., 2023). Leveraging its unique characteristics of immersion, interaction, and imagination, virtual reality is increasingly being applied in healthcare rehabilitation, encompassing areas such as pain management (Zitti et al., 2025), physical rehabilitation (Iqbal et al., 2024), and psychological therapy (Hannigan et al., 2023). In recent years, virtual reality technology has been increasingly implemented in clinical intensive care unit settings. Evidence indicates that providing mechanically ventilated patients with immersive natural landscape experiences offers a promising non-pharmacological avenue for anxiety management (Haley and Wacker, 2022). Meanwhile, interactive virtual reality-based upper limb rehabilitation training for long-term bedridden patients demonstrates considerable potential for functional recovery in critically ill populations (Haghedooren et al., 2024). We postulate that the advantages of virtual reality over conventional care may derive from two key mechanisms: 1) its capacity to redirect patient attention away from intensive care unit stressors through fully immersive pleasant experiences, thereby disrupting the anxiety-pain cycle; and 2) its ability to provide early cognitive and motor activation within a safe, controlled virtual environment, countering the trajectory of functional disuse.

Recent systematic reviews have primarily focused on quantitative outcomes, evaluating the effectiveness of virtual reality for symptom relief in intensive care unit patients (He et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2025). However, existing evidence fails to comprehensively examine the feasibility of implementing virtual reality technology within the complex intensive care unit environment or to adequately capture patients' acceptance and subjective experiences. These dimensions represent crucial determinants for successfully translating this innovative intervention from clinical research into routine practice. Therefore, this mixed-methods systematic review aims to comprehensively and critically synthesize and analyze the existing evidence. It will examine the feasibility, effectiveness and patient experiences of virtual reality in intensive care unit settings, thereby clarifying its application value and identifying potential issues. The findings are expected to provide a scientific foundation for clinical practice and guide future research.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: In this mixed-methods systematic review, we aimed to identify, appraise, and synthesize evidence regarding the feasibility, effectiveness and patient experience of virtual reality among adult intensive care unit patients. The review protocol was prospectively registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO 2025 CRD420251010718). A convergent segregated approach was employed, allowing quantitative and qualitative data to be analyzed separately before integration, following guidance outlined in the JBI Manual for Evidence Synthesis (Stern et al., 2020). The conduct and reporting of this review adhere to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 statement (Page et al., 2021).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p:
The inclusion criteria are summarized in Table 1. We included original English-language studies of all designs, such as randomized controlled trials, quasi-experimental studies, prospective studies, mixed-methods studies, and qualitative studies. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) studies for which the full text could not be retrieved; (2) duplicate publications; (3) studies not published in English or Chinese; and (4) study protocols, case reports, conference abstracts, and reviews.Table 1
Inclusion criteria based on the PICo framework.Table 1 dummy alt text
PICo ElementInclusion Criteria
P (Population)•
Adults (aged 18 or older)•
Any reason for admission or previous intensive care unit stay•
Received treatment in intensive care unit for >24 h
I (Phenomenon of Interest)•
The use of virtual reality-based therapeutic interventions or psychotherapy•
Exclude: Non-VR based interventions
Co (Context)•
Setting: Interventions delivered during ICU stay or after ICU discharge•
Study design: Quantitative studies (experimental and observational); Qualitative studies; Mixed-methods studies•
Outcome measures: Any outcome measuring the impact of virtual reality was included. These outcomes include but are not limited to the following: Satisfaction or acceptance of the use of interventions; Psychological status; Status of limb function recovery; Sleep quality; Barriers and facilitators of use•
Language: English•
Time: Studies identified from database inception to 19 March 2025
Abreviations: VR, virtual reality; ICU, intensive care unit.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: Two investigators (Guiling Liao and Zhiqun Liu) collaboratively developed the systematic search strategy. The formulation of the eligibility criteria and the search strategy was guided by the PICo (Population, Phenomenon of Interest, Context) framework (Lockwood et al., 2015).

A comprehensive literature search was executed across five electronic databases: EMBASE (Ovid), CINAHL (EBSCO), PubMed, the Cochrane Library, and Web of Science. The search incorporated a combination of controlled vocabulary (e.g., subject headings) and free-text keywords to maximize retrieval. The key search concepts were ‘Virtual Reality’ and ‘Intensive Care Unit.’ The search encompassed all publications from the inception of each database up to March 19, 2025, and was limited to studies published in the English language. The detailed, full electronic search strategy for all databases is provided in Supplementary File 1.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: The literature was imported into Endnote X9 software for deduplication, initial screening, and re-screening of the full text. Two investigators (Guiling Liao and Zhiqun Liu) independently screened the literature according to eligibility criteria, and a third investigator (Huan Wan) was sought in case of disagreement.

Each study that met the inclusion criteria was reviewed, and data were extracted independently by the two reviewers. A pre-defined data extraction form was developed based on panel discussions with clinical experts. The two reviewers used this form to extract the following data from the included studies: (1) fundamental study characteristics, including author, publication year, country, setting, study aim, and design; and (2) descriptive data concerning sample characteristics, methodology, outcome measures, and key findings.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (2018 version) was used to assess the quality of the included studies (Hong et al., 2018). The Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool includes two screening questions and five specific criteria for each of the five types of study designs to assess method quality, with options "yes", "no", or "cannot tell". In the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool revision (2018), the authors do not encourage the use of overall numerical ratings to reflect the quality of the study, but provide detailed descriptions of quality criteria ratings (Hong et al., 2018). The quality of the study was rated as high (meeting all criteria), medium (meeting four out of five criteria) or low (meeting three or fewer). In line with the official guidance for the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool provided by Hong et al., (2018), we did not exclude any studies based solely on their quality appraisal scores. To ensure rigor, two researchers (Guiling Liao and Zhiqun Liu) conducted the assessment independently and a third researcher (Huan Wan) mediated any differences.

sec.title: Data synthesis and analysis
sec.p: This mixed-methods systematic review used convergent separation approach to synthesize quantitative and qualitative data. Data synthesis followed the Joanna Briggs Institute methodological guidance for mixed-methods systematic reviews (Aromataris et al., 2024). We first analyzed the interventions, outcomes, and conclusions from quantitative studies. Subsequently, we synthesized these findings with qualitative evidence where possible, linking and organizing the data to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the effectiveness and acceptability of virtual reality technology interventions (Stern et al., 2020). A meta-analysis of quantitative findings was deemed inappropriate due to substantial heterogeneity across clinical populations, intervention characteristics, study designs, and outcome measurements. Therefore, narrative synthesis is used to summarize evidence for each category (Stern et al., 2020). All qualitative studies, including qualitative data derived from other research designs, were systematically analyzed through inductive coding and thematic analysis to identify patterns and address the review's research questions (Clarke and Braun, 2017). In the classification process, the findings are grouped according to their relevance and common meaning, with each category accompanied by a description of the findings and an explanatory statement. The two researchers reviewed the generated categories through logical reasoning, made necessary revisions to the emerging themes, and created a comprehensive synthesis of results (O’Cathain et al., 2010).

sec.title: Result
sec.p:
The initial database search yielded 978 articles. After removing 344 duplicates, 634 titles and abstracts were screened for relevance. The full text of 89 studies was screened, and 19 studies met the inclusion criteria. The flow chart of this process is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
PRISMA flowchart. Abbreviation: PRISMA, preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses.Fig 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: Of the 19 included studies, the methodological designs comprised qualitative studies (n = 2, 11 %), quantitative studies (n = 14, 73 %), and mixed-methods studies (n = 3, 16 %). The enrolled populations were recruited from a range of intensive care settings, including general intensive care unit (n = 10), cardiac intensive care unit (n = 4), and surgical intensive care unit (n = 5).

The 19 included studies addressed three overarching domains: (1) safety and feasibility of virtual reality application in the intensive care unit; and (2) effectiveness of virtual reality on anxiety, pain, sleep quality, cognition and physical function; and (3) patient-experience outcomes such as comfort, satisfaction and perceived pleasantness. Instruments ranged from investigator-designed structured or semi-structured questionnaires to validated scales; some trials relied on a single measure, whereas others applied several concurrently.

Safety and feasibility were monitored in eight trials that documented adverse events during virtual-reality sessions, including line dislodgement, technical malfunctions, falls or transient vital-sign anomalies. One investigation further quantified feasibility by recording the number of sessions completed, actual training time, session efficiency and patient adherence (de Vries et al., 2025).

Anxiety and pain were the most frequently evaluated endpoints. Seven studies evaluated both outcomes concurrently: three applied a Visual Analogue Scale to mood, anxiety and pain (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024); one combined the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale with the Defense and Veterans Pain Rating Scale (Ong et al., 2020); one paired the objective Analgesia Nociception Index with a self-report numeric rating scale (Laghlam et al., 2021); one used the Numeric Pain Rating Scale together with the Faces Anxiety Scale (Yesilot et al., 2022); additionally, one study explored improvements in anxiety and pain through a semi-structured questionnaire (Jawed et al., 2021). Single-outcome assessments were also reported: one study evaluated anxiety exclusively with a Visual Analogue Scale-Anxiety subscale (Haley and Wacker, 2022), another assessed pain only with a visual analogue scale-pain subscale (Parke et al., 2020), and one investigation quantified anxiety with both the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale and the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (Li et al., 2024). Additionally, a study employed the Numeric Rating Scale to assess pain (Naef et al., 2023).

Sleep quality was examined in three trials using either the Richards-Campbell Sleep Questionnaire or the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (Lee and Kang, 2020; Li et al., 2024; Ong et al., 2020); one of these studies also incorporated measures of somatic symptoms, health-related quality of life and cognitive function to provide a broader assessment of virtual reality efficacy (Li et al., 2024).

Patient experiences, including acceptance, comfort, satisfaction, and sense of presence, were primarily evaluated using both researcher-developed questionnaires and pre-validated scales. Two studies assessed patient acceptance and immersion using the Igroup Presence Questionnaire (Gerber et al., 2019; Vlake et al., 2021), while also evaluating discomfort symptoms such as dizziness during operation with the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (Gerber et al., 2019; Vlake et al., 2021). In two other studies, fatigue and other discomforts were measured using the Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion scale, while satisfaction and enjoyment were assessed with a Visual Analogue Scale (Haghedooren et al., 2024; de Vries et al., 2025). One of these studies utilized a modified version of the Borg-RPE scale (Haghedooren et al., 2024). Additionally, two studies employed self-developed questionnaires to evaluate patients' overall satisfaction with the virtual reality intervention (Haghedooren et al., 2024; Naef et al., 2023). Detailed characteristics of the included studies are presented in Table 2, Table 3.Table 2

High availability, high immersion, and very low discomfort.•

One hundred eighty-eight modules were completed by 20 participants.•
The participants completed a median of 9 (SD 4) modules per person. Five participants (25 %) completed all 14 modules.•
All participants completed the activity in a seated position. Mean intervention time was 29 (SD 10) minutes.•
Feasibility: no pipeline detachment, fall or abnormal vital signs occurred; technical problems affected 25 % of the modules (mainly due to environmental interference).•
Patient experience: 95 % thought the activity was comfortable, safe and interesting; 60 % stopped early due to fatigue.•
Quantitative results: 5 patients completed all modules, and the median pain score was 1–3.•
Contradiction: Despite frequent technical problems, patient satisfaction is still high.

9.(Vlake et al. 2021)NetherlandsICUTo explore the determinants of patient-related ICU-specific virtual reality and to explore the impact of ICU-specific virtual reality on mental health.Randomised controlled trialConsecutive mechanically ventilated patients with sepsis or septic shock.50(ICU-VR group:25;control VR group:25)ICU-VR group:15:10;control VR group:14:11ICU-VR group:61 (23–75);control VR group:59 (59–80)•Standardized scales Impact of Event Scale-Revised (Weiss, 2007), Beck Depression Inventory (Upton, 2013), Short Form 12 (Ware et al. 1996),European Quality of Life 5D (EuroQol Group, 1990) assess mental and quality of life.•Immersive:Tendencies Questionnaire (Johns et al. 2000) and Igroup Presence Questionnaire (Schubert 2003)assess immersion•Discomfortableness: Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (Kennedy et al. 1993b) assess vertigo.•Wilcoxon Mann-Whitney U test, Fisher exact test.•ICU-specific virtual reality group accepts 6 module ICU environment simulation.•The control virtual reality group selects natural scenes.•Each intervention lasts about 20 min and is provided once a day, with patients choosing how many times to participate.•ICU-specific virtual reality is a feasible and acceptable new intervention for ICU rehabilitation after critical illness.
10.(Haley and Wacker 2022)United StatesICUWhether mobile-based virtual reality therapy can be used for anxiety management in patients on mechanical ventilation.Prospective observational feasibility studyPatients105:558 (49–66)M•Safety indicators: adverse events such as pipeline detachment and arrhythmia were recorded.•Anxiety assessment: VAS-A.•Standard descriptive and comparative statistical; Wilcoxon signedrank test.•The subjects received at least one virtual reality treatment in which they watched a movie video of an outdoor green or blue space with a 360 visual range.•Goal session duration was 5 min.•Safety: No adverse events (e.g., tube dislodgement) or motion sickness were reported during 18 VR interventions.•Anxiety relief: the median VAS-A score decreased by 8.5 points (p = 0.012), and systolic and diastolic blood pressure were significantly reduced.

12.(Naef et al. 2023)SwitzerlandICU and IMCUThe aim of this study was to determine the response of critically ill patients to repeated immersive virtual reality conversations.Quasi‐experiment studyIntensive care unit patient4520:2557.73 ± 15.92•Physiological parameters: Heart rate and blood pressure were continuously recorded by standard monitoring equipment (Carescape Monitor B650).•Questionnaire survey :A 10-point scale was designed to assess comfort, discomfort and perception of the real environment.•Pain scores:Numeric rating scale was used to assess pain levels before and after VR intervention and 30 min later.•Python 3.10.9 (Python Software Foundation, Wilmington, Delaware, USA) and R for Statistics 4.2.3 (The R Foundation, Vienna, Vienna, Austria).•Play 30 min of 360-degree natural scene video (such as a river, vineyard) with noise-canceling headphones and a head-mounted display (Pico G2 4 K), overlaid with natural sounds (water flowing, birds chirping).•The patient received two VR interventions during ICU hospitalization, each for at least 20 min, with a 24-hour interval between them, and the video playback order was random.•In the two VR interventions, heart rate and blood pressure decreased significantly (p < 0.001).•Subjective perception: high comfort, the perception of the real environment is significantly reduced, only a slight perception of noise.•Pain score: The pain score decreased significantly after the second VR intervention (p = 0.037), but did not reach a clinically significant difference.•Feasibility: 40/54 patients completed two interventions, and the withdrawal was mainly due to health deterioration or discomfort, without serious adverse events.
13.(Haghedooren et al. 2024)BelgiumSICUTo investigate the feasibility and safety of VRR as a rehabilitation incentive tool for patients with long-term hospitalization in ICU.Quasi‐experiment studyPatients205:1548 (min: 19, max: 77)•Feasibility: To be evaluated by recording the time required to set up and guide, use, stop and clean the device, and reporting all (medical, technical/and/or emotional) problems that delay this process.•Safety: monitoring of heart rate, respiratory rate, blood oxygen saturation, blood pressure and adverse events (e.g. catheter dislodgement).•Questionnaire survey :Uncomfort (modified Borg CR10 score), pleasure (VAS), and satisfaction.•Descriptive statistics•Inductive content analysis•Content: Use Meta Quest 2 headset and customized VR application ("MotiVeR UZeLF"), which includes drawing games (upper limb activity) and memory games (cognitive participation), supplemented by virtual relaxation environment.•Implementation: Patients received 3 VR sessions per week for 15 min (up to 30 min) for 3–9 weeks. The physical therapist adjusted the difficulty and environment of the game in real time.•Safety: No major or minor adverse events occurred in 79 VR training sessions, and physiological parameters remained stable.•Feasibility: In addition to one patient experiencing mild nausea and discomfort that may be related to VRR treatment, the rest of the reported problems were technical equipment problems, unrelated to the treatment, and most patients expressed a preference for VRR as a rehabilitation treatment.•Patients reported high fun scores (median 9/10) and physical therapists recognized practicality.•Technical issues: 7.6 % of sessions were delayed by Bluetooth /WiFi connections, and a small number of patients reported that the headset was too heavy or foggy.•Motivation difference: 43.7 % of patients thought VR was as good as traditional rehabilitation motivation, and 37.4 % thought VR was a little boring after repetition.
14.Yuanyuan Li, 2024 (Li et al. 2024)ChinaICUTo study the effectiveness of VR-CBT in anxiety symptoms in patients with AMI admitted to the ICU.Randomized Controlled TrialAMIpatients148 (VR-CBT group:70;control group:78)VR-CBT group:10:60; control group:11:67VR-CBT group:58.3;control group:59.9•The primary outcome was changes in anxiety symptoms assessed with the Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale (Hamilton, 1959) scores.•Secondary outcomes were State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (Julian, 2011), Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (Buysse et al. 1989), Patient Health Questionnaire-15 (Kroenke et al. 2002), Short Form-12 (Ware et al. 1996), Perceived Deficits Questionnaire for Depression.•SPSS 27.0:t-test, Fisher’s exact test.•Both the intervention group and the control group received standard mental health support.•The intervention group also performed VR-CBT for 30 min a day for 7 consecutive days, including 4 modules (hypnotherapy, mindfulness cognition, music therapy, relaxation therapy), which were implemented at the bedside through HMD.•The Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale score of VR-CBT group decreased by 57.3 % compared with the baseline; self-assessed anxiety, sleep quality, somatic symptoms and mental health quality of life were significantly improved (p < 0.001); •Except for Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale, all other improvements had dissipated by the 3-month follow-up.
15.(Locke et al. 2024)United StatesICUThe aim was to evaluate the acceptability and potential utility of immersive VR in critically ill patient.Mixed methods studyPatients35 (Participants: 20; Non-participants: 15)Participants: 5:15;Non-participants:6:9Participants: 61; Non-participants:54•VAS about their mood, anxiety levels and pain.•Physiological indicators measurement.•Paired 2-sided t tests;•Thematic analysis.•Immersive VR experience for 5–15 min, patients can choose three scenes (urban travel, natural landscape, synthetic landscape).•Scales assessment: VAS•Semistructured interview•Severe patients can be exposed to immersive VR for a relatively short period of time.•Improved pain, anxiety and overall mood scores.•No side effects or major technical challenges were generated.
16.(Twamley et al. 2024)United KingdomICUThe aim is to explore the barriers and facilitators of VR exposure therapy in critically ill patients and clinical staff.Qualitative studyPatients and health care professional13(Patients:9;health care professiona:4)NGNG•Focus group discussions were conducted online.•Thematic Analysis;•NVivo (Version 12).•VRET simulates ICU environment, including relaxation scenarios and exposure to ICU scenarios, guided by ICU consultants and psychologists.•Focus group discussion.•Obstacles: psychological fear, sensory discomfort (e.g., vertigo), environmental limitations (e.g., lack of space), and inadequate technical skills of medical staff.•Promoting factors: training of health care workers, patient assessment, technical guidance and support for interventions (e.g. family accompaniment).
17.(de Vries et al. 2025)NetherlandsICUThe aim was to examine the feasibility of using VR headsets for VR treatment during ICU and subsequent admission to general wards.Quasi‐experiment studyPatients103:7Median age was 71 [63–79]•Indicators of feasibility: number of courses, actual training time, course efficiency, compliance and adverse events;•Patient experience: degree of fatigue (Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion Scale (Colberg et al. 2003)) , degree of satisfaction (VAS) ;•Physical function parameters: grip strength (handheld dynamometer) Morton Mobility Index (Sommers et al. 2016).•Qualitative description; •SPSS 24.0: Descriptive statistics,non-parametric, Wilcoxon signed-rank tests.•Tool: Oculus Quest 2® headset, custom VR jigsaw puzzle game (increasing difficulty, requires upper limb activity).•Frequency: 3 times a week for 20 min each time for 4 weeks or until discharge.•Feasibility: Patients completed 3 courses per week on average, with actual training time of 13 min and course efficiency of 57 %.•Adherence 60 %, no serious adverse events reported.•High patient satisfaction.•Improved physical function:Morton Mobility Index scores were significantly improved (p = 0.005), and grip strength was not significantly changed (p > 0.05).
18.(Ju et al. 2025)ChinaSICUTo evaluate the feasibility, acceptability, and potential outcomes of virtual reality (VR) to enhance patient experience during SICU hospitalization.Mixed methods studyUndergoing thoracic surgery participated3211:2160.83±12.25•VAS, used to assess pain, fatigue, depression, anxiety and comfort.•The self-designed questionnaire was used to evaluate VR acceptance•Visually Induced Motion Sickness Susceptibility Questionnaire (Golding et al. 2021) was used to evaluate discomfort related to VR intervention•In-depth interviews.•SPSS 29.0; paired t-tests; Wilcoxon signed-rank test•Colaizzi’s seven-step method.•VR experience: 6 min and 15 s of natural landscapes (such as waterfalls, beaches and forests) and further relaxation through voice-guided meditation.•Interviews;Questionnaires.•VR intervention significantly reduced the psychological pressure of SICU patients, improved their comfort, and had high acceptance and minimal side effects.• The intervention resulted in significant reductions in pain, fatigue, depression, and anxiety, alongside improved comfort levels (p < 0.05).·No significant changes in blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, or SpO₂.·High VR acceptability (mean score: 3.90 ± 0.62) with minimal discomfort.·Qualitative Themes: Positive patient attitude toward VR; Benefits of VR for patient well-being;Multiple influences on VR implementation;Implementing VR with safety assurance.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool ratings indicated that four studies (Laghlam et al., 2021; Lee and Kang, 2020; Navarra-Ventura et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2025) met all five quality criteria (4/19, 21 %), eight (Ju et al., 2025; Li et al., 2024; Locke et al., 2024; Ong et al., 2020; Twamley et al., 2024; Vlake et al., 2021; de Vries et al., 2025; Yesilot et al., 2022) satisfied four criteria (8/19, 42 %), and seven (Gerber et al., 2019; Haghedooren et al., 2024; Haley and Wacker, 2022; Jawed et al., 2021; Jones, 2020; Naef et al., 2023; Parke et al., 2020) fulfilled three or fewer (7/19, 37 %). Both qualitative investigations (2/19, 11 %) drew comprehensive conclusions from their datasets (Twamley et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025). Among the eight (Gerber et al., 2019; Haghedooren et al., 2024; Haley and Wacker, 2022; Jawed et al., 2021; Naef et al., 2023; Ong et al., 2020; Parke et al., 2020; de Vries et al., 2025) non-randomised quantitative studies (8/19, 42 %), the principal concern was potential between-group bias attributable to absence of randomisation. Although the study provides valuable insights into the feasibility and preliminary effects of the virtual reality intervention, its findings should be interpreted with caution.

In three mixed-methods reports (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024) integration was incomplete: divergent or contradictory quantitative and qualitative findings were not explicitly reconciled, and procedural detail on data integration was scant. One of these trials (Ju et al., 2025) adopted a sequential-explanatory design, using semi-structured interviews after the quantitative phase to elaborate patients’ virtual reality experiences; the two datasets were subsequently merged to interpret overall results. Table 5 shows details of the quality assessment of the studies included. All studies, regardless of methodological quality, were included in the synthesis.

sec.title: Virtual reality protocols: content, dose and delivery characteristics
sec.p: All 19 researches employed virtual reality interventions, yet the operational details—video content, session length, frequency and overall protocol—varied widely. Immersive virtual reality, primarily implemented through head-mounted displays, constituted the predominant modality across the included studies. Only two investigations (Navarra-Ventura et al., 2021; Parke et al., 2020) adopted non-immersive, screen-based systems.

Five studies delivered psychological therapy through virtual reality. Three of these combined immersive scenes with guided meditation (Ju et al., 2025; Lee and Kang, 2020; Ong et al., 2020), whereas two integrated neuro-cognitive stimulation tasks with the virtual environment (Li et al., 2024; Navarra-Ventura et al., 2021). Virtual reality was also embedded in early mobilisation programmes. In four investigations, patients wearing head-mounted displays performed video-game-based exercises that promoted upper- and lower-limb movement; sessions were scheduled three times per week and were intentionally challenging, eliciting measurable physical exertion (Haghedooren et al., 2024; Parke et al., 2020; de Vries et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025).

The commonest application, however, was relaxation and anxiolysis. Ten studies tailored virtual reality content to patient preferences, combining thematic visual environments with accompanying music. Virtual environments primarily featured natural landscapes (e.g., mountains, oceans, forests), urban scenery, or digitally synthesized settings. These 360-degree panoramas rotated according to the participant’s gaze, creating a sense of immersion, and were often accompanied by classical music or delivered with noise-cancelling headphones. This audiovisual package was intended to distract from pain and foster a sense of presence in a restorative environment.

Regarding session duration, all but one study (Vlake et al., 2021) reported the length of individual virtual reality sessions, which ranged from 5 to 45 min. The majority of sessions lasted between 5 and 20 min. Table 4 lists the detailed characteristics of the virtual reality interventions in the included studies.Table 4

sec.title: Feasibility and safety of virtual reality
sec.p: This review assessed feasibility through patient completion rates, adherence, and technical issues during the implementation of virtual reality interventions. The overall feasibility of virtual reality in the intensive care unit setting is supported, although technical and adherence-related challenges remain.

A quasi-experimental study (Naef et al., 2023) reported that 74 % (40/54) of patients completed both intervention sessions. Another virtual reality rehabilitation study (de Vries et al., 2025) indicated that patients adhered to 60 % of the virtual reality treatment sessions. Reasons for non-adherence included fatigue (11 %), lack of motivation (11 %), perceived lack of value of virtual reality (17 %), and technical difficulties (3 %).

Technical issues were identified as a key factor affecting feasibility. One study (Parke et al., 2020) highlighted that technical errors, such as environmental interference or device malfunctions, occurred in 25 % of the rehabilitation modules, and staff assistance was required in 36 % of the modules. In another study (Haghedooren et al., 2024), physical therapists reported six instances of Bluetooth/ WiFi connection problems during virtual reality therapy, along with other technical issues such as headset displacement, fogging, excessive weight or tightness, and excessive screen brightness.

Regarding safety, none of the included studies reported serious adverse events related to virtual reality interventions, and vital signs remained within normal ranges throughout. A study involving 20 intensive care unit patients (Parke et al., 2020) reported no incidents of falls, catheter dislodgement, or other medical adverse events during 188 rehabilitation modules. Another study (Haghedooren et al., 2024) observed no major adverse events across 79 virtual reality rehabilitation sessions; only one mild case of cybersickness was reported in a patient who already had nausea symptoms, occurring in 1.3 % of sessions. The most frequently reported mild discomforts included dizziness, eye strain, and fatigue.

sec.title: Effectiveness of virtual reality
sec.p: Immersive virtual reality interventions demonstrate significant effects in improving mental health among intensive care unit patients, though their impact on physiological symptoms is heterogeneous.

In anxiety management, seven of nine studies (Haley and Wacker, 2022; Jawed et al., 2021; Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Li et al., 2024; Locke et al., 2024; Ong et al., 2020) reported positive outcomes. A study on virtual reality based cognitive behavioral therapy (Li et al., 2024) found that patients with acute myocardial infarction who received the intervention showed significantly reduced anxiety scores after treatment (β = −9.38, 95 % CI: −11.55 to −7.22, p < 0.001), and this effect remained statistically significant at the 3-month follow-up (β = −2.18, 95 % CI: −4.08 to −0.28, p = 0.024). Similarly, a mixed-methods study (Locke et al., 2024) reported that a single virtual reality session reduced anxiety scores by an average of 1.7 points (95 % CI 0.8–2.7; P = 0.001). These results suggest that virtual reality can rapidly alleviate anxiety in the short term, and that structured virtual reality cognitive behavioral therapy over one week may help prevent long-term persistence of anxiety symptoms. However, the evidence is not entirely consistent; two randomized controlled trials (Laghlam et al., 2021; Yesilot et al., 2022) reported no statistically significant difference in anxiety levels between the virtual reality and control groups (p > 0.05).

Regarding pain management, study results are conflicting. One randomized controlled trial (Yesilot et al., 2022) found that patients who received a single 30-minute virtual reality intervention in the surgical intensive care unit showed significantly lower Numeric Pain Rating Scale scores compared to the control group immediately after the intervention (p<0.001). Three other mixed-methods studies (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024) also reported statistically significant pain reduction following virtual reality intervention (p < 0.05). In contrast, two prospective studies (Jawed et al., 2021; Ong et al., 2020) observed no significant effects, with one study (Jawed et al., 2021) noting that 57 % of patients experienced no change in pain or discomfort. One study (Naef et al., 2023) reported a statistically significant reduction in pain scores after the second session (p = 0.037), but the change did not reach clinical significance.

In terms of sleep quality, two of three studies (Lee and Kang, 2020; Li et al., 2024; Ong et al., 2020)—both randomized controlled trials (Lee and Kang, 2020; Li et al., 2024)—indicated that the virtual reality group reported significantly better subjective sleep quality than the control group. However, in one of these trials (Li et al., 2024), the medium to long-term (3-month) effect was not significant, suggesting that extended or repeated interventions may be needed to maintain benefits. One prospective study (Ong et al., 2020) found no significant effect of virtual reality on sleep.

In the area of physical function rehabilitation, virtual reality technology shows considerable potential. A study focusing on upper limb rehabilitation (de Vries et al., 2025) reported that Morton Mobility Index scores increased significantly from the beginning of the virtual reality training period (median 26 [interquartile range 24–44]) to the end (57 [41–85]) (p = 0.005), indicating improvements in balance and flexibility. Another study (Parke et al., 2020) noted that 90 % of patients believed virtual reality improved muscle strength and joint mobility, and 95 % reported that it motivated them to continue participating in rehabilitation.

In the domain of cognitive intervention, evidence is still preliminary. One study (Gerber et al., 2019) explored perceptions of virtual reality cognitive stimulation and found that virtual reality content was more easily recalled than actual intensive care unit experiences, potentially reducing the risk of post-traumatic stress disorder. Another randomized controlled trial (Navarra-Ventura et al., 2021) showed that patients who received non-immersive virtual reality based neurocognitive stimulation had better working memory scores at one-month follow-up (p = 0.009, d = 0.363).

sec.title: Patient experience from quantitative studies
sec.p: Quantitative measures from standardized scales consistently indicate a high level of acceptance and satisfaction with virtual reality interventions among intensive care unit patients. One study (Haghedooren et al., 2024) reported a median enjoyment score of 9 (interquartile range 8–10, out of 10) after each session. Another study found that 86 % of patients considered the virtual reality device comfortable, and 79 % gave an overall experience score of 3 or higher on a 5-point scale (Jawed et al., 2021). A quasi-experimental study (Naef et al., 2023) also recorded high comfort scores among participants [median (interquartile range) = 8 (7, 10); mean = 8.06 ± 2.31], with low levels of discomfort reported [median (interquartile range) = 1 (1, 1); mean = 1.11 ± 0.62].

Regarding immersion, a randomized controlled trial (Vlake et al., 2021) showed that patients exposed to intensive care unit-specific virtual reality content experienced a greater sense of immersion compared to the control group exposed to a natural virtual reality environment, along with lower cybersickness scores and no changes in vital signs. In addition, one study (Ju et al., 2025) indicated improved patient comfort after the virtual reality intervention (P < 0.05), with a mean acceptance score of 3.90 ± 0.62 and minimal reported discomfort. Interestingly, with successive virtual reality sessions, several parameters showed improvement, including session duration, virtual reality game engagement time, treatment efficiency, satisfaction, and fatigue levels (de Vries et al., 2025).

sec.title: Qualitative findings
sec.p: Based on thematic analysis of qualitative components from six studies—including two qualitative studies (Twamley et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025), three mixed-methods studies (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024), and the embedded qualitative segment of one prospective study (Ong et al., 2020)—three thematic findings were identified. The qualitative findings provided critical depth, explaining, complementing, and at times contrasting with the quantitative evidence.

First, regarding feasibility and safety, qualitative data expanded the understanding beyond high completion rates. Patients and staff reported practical implementation barriers, such as technical difficulties, device discomfort, and environmental constraints within the intensive care unit.

Second, concerning effectiveness, qualitative findings elucidated the mechanisms behind quantitative outcomes. For anxiety reduction, patients described the experience as providing a sense of “calm,” “distraction,” and being “transported away,” directly explaining the significant reductions measured in scales. Regarding the heterogeneous quantitative results for pain management, qualitative accounts revealed a key distinction: Virtual reality was more effective as a distractor from the affective component of pain and associated distress, rather than for severe nociceptive pain itself. This explains the variability in efficacy across studies and patient types.

Third, related to patient experience, qualitative data revealed a layer of nuance not captured by high acceptability scores alone. While patients valued the psychological escape, sense of control, and increased motivation, they simultaneously expressed a strong desire for personalized content and adjustable experiences. This indicates that high satisfaction scores represent an aggregate positive impression, which can be further enhanced through tailored intervention design.

Detailed supporting quotations and study-by-study data corresponding to these integrated insights are presented in Table 6.Table 6

Abreviations: VR, Virtual Reality.

sec.title: Integration of quantitative and qualitative findings
sec.p: Through a convergent segregated approach, we integrated quantitative and qualitative findings to develop a multidimensional understanding of virtual reality application in the intensive care unit. The quantitative and qualitative findings from the included studies were generally complementary, with the qualitative results providing essential depth and context beyond the quantitative evidence.

Regarding feasibility, the completion rates indicated by quantitative data were interconnected with the technical operational difficulties, device comfort issues, and spatial constraints reported in qualitative studies. The considerable variation in session efficiency observed in virtual reality rehabilitation studies was explained by patients' qualitative feedback concerning individual differences, fatigue levels, and varying technological adaptability. This integration suggests that feasibility is not merely a technical issue but also involves human-device interaction and individualized matching.

Concerning effectiveness, quantitative evidence demonstrated that virtual reality significantly improved anxiety and pain scores, while qualitative data revealed its mechanism of action: achieving attention transfer and environmental escape through immersive experiences. For example, the significant reduction in anxiety scores (p < 0.001) in a virtual reality-based cognitive behavioral therapy study was corroborated by patient descriptions of "temporarily forgetting worries" and "focusing on the images before them," forming a triangulation of evidence. Regarding pain management, the heterogeneity in quantitative findings was partially explained by qualitative insights, indicating that the attention-diverting effect of virtual reality may vary due to differences in individual pain perception and content preferences.

Regarding the overall experience, quantitative acceptance scores and qualitative descriptions of immersion and "unique experiences" provided a complementary understanding. High acceptance stemmed not only from the technical effects themselves but also from the psychological escape and sense of control offered by virtual reality. However, the qualitative data also revealed challenges concealed behind high scores, such as device discomfort and operational difficulties, which could not be captured by quantitative assessment alone.

This integrated analysis constructs a comprehensive framework that reveals the dual nature of virtual reality in the intensive care unit: it is both an intervention tool with significant therapeutic potential and a technology facing multiple implementation challenges. Successful virtual reality implementation requires balancing standardization with personalization while simultaneously considering the unity of technical feasibility and patient experience, providing important guidance for future clinical practice and research.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This is the first mixed-methods systematic review to synthesise quantitative and qualitative evidence on the feasibility, effectiveness and patient experience of virtual reality for critically ill adults.

Our findings indicate that virtual reality, as an emerging adjunct therapeutic tool, demonstrates significant potential in alleviating anxiety, improving sleep quality, facilitating early-phase rehabilitation, and enhancing cognitive function among patients. However, its efficacy in pain management among intensive care unit patients remains controversial. The application of virtual reality technology in the intensive care unit setting appears generally safe, with no serious adverse events reported, and patients consistently report positive experiences. Nevertheless, its successful implementation is highly dependent on the precise understanding of individual patient differences and the effective management of implementation challenges.

sec.title: Feasibility and safety of virtual reality interventions
sec.p: This mixed-methods systematic review indicates that virtual reality is generally feasible and demonstrates a favorable safety profile in intensive care unit settings, though its successful implementation faces multiple challenges spanning technical, human-computer interaction, and individualization dimensions.

Regarding feasibility, quantitative studies revealed variability in completion rates and adherence to virtual reality interventions. One study reported that 74 % of patients completed two sessions (Naef et al., 2023), while another rehabilitation study noted patients adhered to only 60 % of scheduled sessions, with reasons for non-adherence including fatigue, lack of motivation, and technical difficulties (de Vries et al., 2025). Notably, as interventions progressed, overly simplistic or monotonous content emerged as a new factor contributing to declining adherence, strongly suggesting that virtual reality content requires dynamic adjustment and personalization to maintain long-term patient engagement (de Vries et al., 2025). Qualitative data further revealed three major barriers to feasibility: technical complexity (particularly for elderly patients), inadequate device comfort (including dizziness and cumbersome equipment), and environmental constraints (limited operational space). These qualitative and quantitative findings collectively indicate that enhancing feasibility requires improvements in three key areas: optimizing user-friendly technical design, developing personalized and dynamically updated virtual reality content to sustain patient engagement, and significantly improving the physical comfort of devices to reduce physical burden.

Regarding safety, no included studies reported serious adverse events, and vital signs remained stable throughout interventions. The incidence of minor adverse reactions (such as cybersickness) was low (1.3 %) (Haghedooren et al., 2024), attributed to multiple protective measures implemented across studies: professional supervision, individualized difficulty adjustment, seated training, and patient autonomy to discontinue sessions (Haghedooren et al., 2024). Patient concerns about collision risks expressed in qualitative studies also highlight the need for thorough environmental safety assessments.

In summary, the application of virtual reality in intensive care units demonstrates controllable safety risks, though widespread implementation requires overcoming technical, experiential, and environmental barriers. Future efforts should focus on developing virtual reality systems better suited for critically ill patients and establishing standardized clinical implementation protocols.

sec.title: Effectiveness of virtual reality interventions
sec.p: This mixed-methods systematic review demonstrates that virtual reality produces significant benefits for improving mental health and functional recovery in intensive care unit patients, though its effects on pain management show considerable heterogeneity.

In anxiety management, seven of nine studies reported positive outcomes. Single-session or short-term immersive virtual reality interventions rapidly reduced anxiety scores, with structured virtual reality-based cognitive behavioral therapy maintaining effects up to three months post-intervention (Li et al., 2024). Similarly, mixed-methods studies (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024) demonstrated significant anxiety reduction following single virtual reality sessions. These findings align with recent meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials on virtual reality for anxiety management (Cheng et al., 2025). Patient feedback from qualitative studies, such as "took me away from my stress for 10 min" and "felt calm, peaceful, and grounded," elucidates the potential mechanism: immersive experiences facilitate attention redirection, effectively "removing" patients from the stressful intensive care unit environment and disrupting the anxiety cycle. However, two randomized controlled trials found no significant differences, suggesting that intervention characteristics and patient populations may serve as effect modifiers.

Pain management outcomes remain contentious. Four studies (Jones, 2020; Ju et al., 2025; Locke et al., 2024; Yesilot et al., 2022) reported significant pain score reductions following virtual reality intervention, while three studies (Jawed et al., 2021; Naef et al., 2023; Ong et al., 2020) observed no effect, with over half of patients showing no pain improvement (Jawed et al., 2021). Although patients described virtual reality as helping them "forget distressing memories," these subjective benefits did not consistently translate into clinically significant pain score reductions. This suggests virtual reality may be more suitable as an adjunct for moderate pain or within multimodal analgesia regimens rather than as a replacement for potent analgesics.

Regarding functional rehabilitation, virtual reality demonstrates unique advantages. One study observed significant improvement in Morton Mobility Index scores from 26 at baseline to 57, indicating enhanced balance and flexibility (de Vries et al., 2025). This suggests that gamifying rehabilitation exercises can effectively increase patient engagement and training efficacy. This resonates with findings in neurological rehabilitation (e.g., post-stroke) (Mekbib et al., 2021), where virtual reality, by providing immediate feedback and engaging challenges, activates the brain's reward pathways, thereby enhancing motivation and efficiency in motor learning.

Evidence for sleep quality and cognitive intervention, while preliminary, is insightful. Two randomized controlled trials (Lee and Kang, 2020; Li et al., 2024) indicated that virtual reality improved subjective sleep quality, though long-term effects were not significant. Virtual reality-based neurocognitive stimulation shows potential for improving short-term working memory, opening new possibilities for preventing post-intensive care syndrome, though larger studies are needed for validation.

sec.title: Patient experience with virtual reality interventions
sec.p: Virtual reality interventions provided intensive care unit patients with highly positive and immersive experiences, with overall acceptability firmly established.

Multiple studies reported high patient ratings in enjoyment, comfort, and overall experience (Haghedooren et al., 2024; Jawed et al., 2021; Naef et al., 2023). Notably, specifically designed virtual reality content customized for the intensive care unit environment induced stronger immersion while maintaining low risk of cybersickness, providing crucial insights for optimizing patient-centered virtual reality protocols (Vlake et al., 2021). Furthermore, evidence suggests patient adaptation and satisfaction with virtual reality may improve through repeated sessions, indicating potential for sustained application of this technology (de Vries et al., 2025). Qualitative data profoundly illuminated the reasons behind these high ratings. Patients frequently used descriptors such as "transported," "immersive," and "distracting" to characterize their experiences. This profound sense of immersion enabled temporary "escape" from the stressful intensive care unit environment, providing valuable psychological relief and mental rejuvenation.

Beneath this generally positive experience, patients expressed clear needs for personalization. They desired virtual reality content aligned with personal preferences, physical conditions, and tolerance levels (Ju et al., 2025). Some patients highlighted video quality (particularly resolution) as a crucial factor affecting immersion. More importantly, many emphasized the need for flexible interventions adaptable to their fluctuating daily energy levels and health status, rather than adhering to fixed durations or session schedules (Ju et al., 2025). This underscores the importance of approaching patients as active participants rather than passive recipients during virtual reality implementation. Future clinical practice should focus on developing high-quality customized content and implementing flexible, individualized intervention strategies to fully realize the humanized potential of virtual reality technology.

sec.title: Implications for clinical practice
sec.p: This mixed-methods systematic review synthesizes evidence across three key dimensions—feasibility, effectiveness and patient experience—to provide a foundational assessment of virtual reality ’s potential in intensive care unit care. While the aggregated findings are promising, they concurrently highlight that the evidence base remains nascent and heterogeneous. Therefore, rather than recommending immediate clinical standardization, this review underscores the imperative to channel future efforts toward methodological strengthening and translational research to realize virtual reality’s full potential.

To advance the field, future efforts must prioritize strengthening the evidence base and translating findings into practice. This necessitates conducting large-scale, rigorous trials to definitively establish efficacy for specific clinical indications, alongside parallel work to develop standardized, symptom-specific intervention protocols that address safety, feasibility, and implementation barriers. Crucially, the co-design of future virtual reality applications with patients and clinicians, informed by the qualitative insights synthesized herein, will be key to enhancing acceptability and ensuring that technological advancements align with genuine clinical needs and user experiences.

In summary, advancing virtual reality from a promising technology to an evidence-based, routine component of intensive care unit care necessitates a coordinated focus on rigorous evidence generation, protocol development, and implementation science. Collaborative efforts among researchers, clinicians, and patients along these directions will be pivotal in determining the sustainable role of virtual reality in improving intensive care unit patient outcomes.

sec.title: Study limitations
sec.p: Several limitations of this review should be noted. First, the search strategy may have missed relevant synonyms, potentially affecting literature comprehensiveness. Second, by including both intensive care unit and post-intensive care unit settings, we captured a broader recovery continuum but introduced heterogeneity in patient acuity and care environments. These factors, along with significant variability in intervention designs and population characteristics across studies, precluded a meta-analysis and limit generalizability. Third, the overall strength of evidence is constrained by the predominance of small-scale studies with short-term follow-up and moderate methodological quality. Fourth, potential publication bias cannot be excluded. Finally, the evidence is dominated by immersive virtual reality systems, limiting comparisons with non-immersive formats. Notwithstanding these limitations, this review rigorously appraises and synthesizes the existing mixed-methods evidence, providing a foundational and nuanced understanding that specifically informs the design of future, more definitive studies. Direct comparative trials and standardized protocols tailored to specific clinical phases are needed to advance the field.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Virtual reality interventions demonstrate acceptable feasibility and meaningful benefits for critically ill adults, with patients consistently reporting positive experiences. However, the current evidence is limited by small sample sizes, variable methodological quality, heterogeneous hardware, and—importantly—inadequate control for potential confounding factors in several studies. These methodological shortcomings may compromise the validity and generalizability of the findings.

Future research should adopt more rigorous designs, such as adequately powered randomized controlled trials, and employ advanced statistical approaches including multivariable regression and propensity score analysis to better account for confounding variables. Such efforts will help refine delivery protocols, improve device ergonomics and technical reliability, and enable systematic personalization of content based on individual patient preferences. Addressing these priorities will not only maximize the therapeutic potential of virtual reality but also generate the high-certainty evidence necessary to support its widespread implementation in intensive care practice.
